coredata.dc:title: Facile and Green synthesis of fluorescent N-doped carbon dots from betel leaves for sensitive detection of Picric acid and Iron ion
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: K., Kalanidhi
P., Nagaraaj
coredata.dc:description: In this present work, a facile, green, cost effective route has been applied for the synthesis of water soluble carbon dots from betel leaves by hydrothermal route. As obtained nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) show remarkable advantages such as water solubility, biocompatibility and excellent stability. It is analyzed by vital characterization techniques such as HR-TEM, AFM, PSA, XRD, XPS, FTIR, UV-–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy with lifetime analysis. It exhibits blue emission under UV lamp (wavelength 365 nm), excitation dependent emission behavior and independent of pH. The obtained N-CDs show excellent response to picric acid (PA) and Fe3+ ions which leads to fluorescence quenching. This phenomenon was applied to design a single probe fluorescence based system for dual detection with high selectivity and sensitivity. A good linear response was obtained between concentration of analyte and fluorescence intensity with limit of detection (LOD) 0.11μM in the range of 0.3−3.3μM for PA and 0.135μM in the range of 0.3−3.3μM for Fe3+. Finally, the obtained fluorescent probe could be a promising tool for sensitive detection of PA and Fe3+ in aqueous solution.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-carbon dots
Betel leaves
Fluorescence
Picric acid and Fe3+ ions

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Formation of CDs from betel leaves
Effect of pH, storage time and ionic strength

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The author thanks to University Grants Commission, New Delhi, India for providing NFOBC fellowship and the authors also thank to Department of chemistry, Anna University for constant support and encouragement.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HR-TEM images of N-CDs. (a) Higher magnification image, (b) well dispered image, (inset: SAED pattern of CDs) (c) Dispered image with size specifications (d) PSA.
AFM image of N-CDs. (a) 2D topographic image (b) Histogram of NCDs (c) 3D topographic image of N-CDs.
(a) Absorbance spectrum of N-CDs (inset: blue emission under UV lamp.
(b) Absorbance and emission spectrum of N-CDs.
(c) Fluorescence emission spectra of carbon dots at different excitation wavelength ranging from 300−400nm.
FTIR spectrum of N-CDs.
XRD spectrum of N-CD.
(a) XPS spectrum of N-CDs (b) High resolution spectrum of C1s (c) High resolution spectrum of O1s (d) High resolution spectrum of N1s.
Fluorescence quenching efficiency of N-CDs towards different analysts.
(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs with different concentration of PA.
(b) Correlation between emission intensity and concentration of PA.
(c) Linear response between fluorescence intensity and PA concentration.
Fluorescence quenching rate of N-CDs towards different metal ions.
a) Fluorescence emission spectra with different concentration of PA.
b) Correlation between fluorescence intensity and Fe3+ concentration.
c) Linear response curve between concentration of Fe3+ and fluorescence intensity.
Schematic representation of synthesis of carbon dots.
Schematic representation of possible sensing mechanism.
Schematic representation of Fe3+ sensing by CDs.
Comparison of proposed sensing system for PA and Fe3+ detection with previous literature.

row.entry: 1) Multi-hydroxy carbon dots assembled SBA-15/560 composite probe

head.ce:title: Facile and Green synthesis of fluorescent N-doped carbon dots from betel leaves for sensitive detection of Picric acid and Iron ion

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Anna University, Chennai, 600025, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Anna University, Chennai-600025

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this present work, a facile, green, cost effective route has been applied for the synthesis of water soluble carbon dots from betel leaves by hydrothermal route. As obtained nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) show remarkable advantages such as water solubility, biocompatibility and excellent stability. It is analyzed by vital characterization techniques such as HR-TEM, AFM, PSA, XRD, XPS, FTIR, UV-–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy with lifetime analysis. It exhibits blue emission under UV lamp (wavelength 365 nm), excitation dependent emission behavior and independent of pH. The obtained N-CDs show excellent response to picric acid (PA) and Fe3+ ions which leads to fluorescence quenching. This phenomenon was applied to design a single probe fluorescence based system for dual detection with high selectivity and sensitivity. A good linear response was obtained between concentration of analyte and fluorescence intensity with limit of detection (LOD) 0.11μM in the range of 0.3−3.3μM for PA and 0.135μM in the range of 0.3−3.3μM for Fe3+. Finally, the obtained fluorescent probe could be a promising tool for sensitive detection of PA and Fe3+ in aqueous solution.

ce:section.ce:para: Picric acid also referred as 2,4,6 tri nitro phenol (TNP) is widely used as important reagent in organic synthesis, dye industries, pharma and military industries [1,2]. At the same time, PA at very low concentration can induce serious health issues such as eye/skin damage, cancer and respiratory problem as well as it has strong impact on environment due to good water solubility which leads to contamination of soil and ground water [3–6]. Therefore, efficient and accurate method for determination of Picric acid is gaining a significant importance in the field of sensor.

Similarly, heavy metal ions are also playing a crucial role in the environmental pollution and it is considered as major environmental pollutants which causes serious health problems to the environment as well as human beings when it exceeds the optimum level [7,8]. Therefore, designing of sensitive probe is much needed for detection of heavy metal ions in the environment. Recently, fluorescence based detection has scored an increased concern due to its simplicity, high selectivity, sensitivity and fast response [9,10]. In addition to this, many efforts have been made to bring a different and most efficient sensing system with accurate selectivity and sensitivity. In this connection, the previous literatures employed the utilization of various nanomaterials such as Carbon dots [11], graphene oxide [12], metal oxide nanomaterials [13] and polymer dots [14] etc. which improves sensing ability with low detection limit, high sensitivity and selectivity. Among all, most widely explored multispecialty nanomaterial is Carbon dots (CDs), a new class of carbon based nanomaterial, has acquired importance due to its excellent physiochemical properties such as chemical inertness, stability, tunable photoluminescence property, abundant, low cost, ease surface functionalization and biocompatibility [15–17]. These inspiring properties make CDs a potential candidate in different fields such as sensor [18], bio-imaging, fluorescence ink [19] and catalysis [20]. These properties in-turn make CDs, a promising replacement tool to conventional semiconductor quantum dots, polymer dot and organic dye that have been reported in the past. Different precursors have been used for preparation of CDs in the past years.

Recently, various processing routes have been employed to prepare CDs such as Laser ablation, Arc discharge, electrochemical, microwave and hydrothermal [21,22]. Among these process, microwave method has gained less attention due to uncontrolled reaction condition and particle size. Most interestingly, hydrothermal route has been adopted for its simplicity, eco friendliness and no need of toxic chemicals and complicated procedure [23,24].

The key challenge is to enhance the photoluminescence of carbon dots is achieved by doping method in which different heteroatoms such as N, O, S, B and P have been incorporated in crystal lattice or surface of the CDs by creating more binding sites and developing defects as a result of enhanced photoluminescence property can be achieved. Among all, N-doped CDs is studied most to alter the photoluminescence property due to its ease bonding ability with carbon atom [25,26].

Various precursors have been utilized for the preparation of CDs. However, use of green, cost effective method and cheap, ecofriendly raw materials are being recommended for establishing a sustainable material for wider applications and large scale synthesis. Recently, many efforts have been given to carbon dots which are originated from natural resources. G.Vengatesan et al. [27] proposed a green and economical way of synthesizing carbon dots from boswellia ovalifoliolata bark as a carbon source by hydrothermal route for Fe3+ sensing with LOD of 0.41μM. M.Yuvan et al. [28] developed a low cost, greener and sustainable route for producing carbon dots for Fe3+ detection from a cheap and abundant raw material i.e wheat straw as a carbon precursor by hydrothermal route with LOD value of 1.95μM. R.Atchudan et al. [29] demonstrated the utilization of chionanthus retusus fruit as a carbon source and ammonia solution as N-dopant for the production of N-doped carbon dots by simple hydrothermal route for iron detection with LOD of 70μM. Similarly, Z.M.S.H. Khan et al. [30] proposed a green approach for the preparation of carbon dots NS-CQDs from l-Lysine and thiourea as a carbon and nitrogen source respectively. It has been effectively used as a sensing probe for picric acid detection with LOD of 0.24μM. However, the obtained limit of detection (LOD) value for the above proposed green methods was comparatively high. Keeping this in consideration, developing a green and economical route for producing CDs for sensing application with as low as the limit of detection value is of great importance and difficult task.

In this work, we have attempted betel leaves as precursor for the synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots which eliminates the use of cost ineffective and hazardous chemicals and complicated synthetic routes. The botanical name of betel leaf is Piper betle and it belongs to piperaceae family. It is an auspicious leaves, has been used for many health disorders, especially it helps to cure cough, respiratory problems, digestion issues due to its excellent properties such as anti-inflammatory, anti-diabetic and anti-infective etc., [31,32]. Though, they are many reports are available on synthesis of carbon dots from plants extract but the present study is gaining much importance owing to its availability and low cost and can be perfectly eligible for large scale production.

In the present work, we explore a facile, green, cost effective route for preparation of carbon dots from betel leaves and ammonia as a green nitrogen dopant by hydrothermal method. The synthesized N-CDs exhibit blue fluorescence under UV lamp, water solubility and good stability. It shows excellent selectivity and sensitivity towards picric acid and Fe3+. The synthesized N-CDs act as a single probe system for dual sensing application with limit of detection 0.11μM for PA and 0.135μM for Fe3+which implies that the synthesized N-CDs hold the promising properties for sensing of picric acid and Fe3+ in aqueous solution.

Betel leaves were obtained from a local market in Chennai. Picric acid was commercially purchased from LOBA. All organic compounds (Nitrobenzene, Phenol, 4-Nitrophenol, Toluene, Aniline, Benzene, Benzoic acid, Hydroquinone and p-Benzoquinone) were purchased from Merck. All metal salts (FeCl3, ZnSO4, CdCl2, Pb(NO3)2, AgNO3, CuSO4, FeCl2, NaSO4, CuCl2, Cr2O3, CuCl3, ZnCl2, HgCl2 were purchased from sigma-aldrich. All procured reagents were analytical grade and used without further purification. The distilled water was used throughout the work to carry out the experiments.

The absorption measurements were taken from UV–vis spectrophotometer (JASCO). The emission spectra were recorded using Spectrofluorometer (Perkin Emler LS-45). The fluorescence lifetime was measured by Horiba Fluorolog 3 TCSPC. High resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM- Jeol/JEM 2100) helps to analyse the morphology and size of the particles. The 3D topography of the samples was examined through Atomic Force Microscope (WITec alpha300RA, Germany). Particle size analyser (PSA- Horiba,Japan) has been used to determine the size of the synthesized particles. The active functional sites of synthesized compound were analyzed by ATR-IR spectroscopy (frequency range 400−4000 cm−1, BRUKAR). Chemical composition was identified through X-Ray Photon Spectroscopy. Structural analysis was evidenced by X-Ray Diffraction Spectroscopy (XRD, BRUKAR. The quantum yield measurement was recorded in fluorescence spectroscopy (Ocean optics, USD 2000+)

Carbon dots are synthesized from betel leaves as a carbon sources and ammonia solution as a green nitrogen dopant by a single-step hydrothermal process (Scheme 1 ). A typical synthesis procedure is as follows: 5 g of betel leaves was mixed with 50 mL of DI water in which 3 mL of ammonia solution was poured into it. Then it was placed into 100 mL Teflon lined autoclave and kept in hot air oven at 180 °C for 12 h as a result of a dark yellow colored solution was obtained which indicates the formation of N-doped carbon dots. After that it was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 20 min to remove larger particles and non-fluorescent particles. A clear yellow colored solution is collected and filtered using 0.22 μm membrane filter paper. Then the obtained solution was stored at 4 °C for future use.

The detection of picric acid was done by spectrofluorometer. 10 μL of prepared N-CDs (PH.7) in 2.9 mL of distilled water was taken in a cuvette and 100 μL of different concentration of picric acid were added to it. Then the solution was allowed to stand for 5 min. to enable complete interaction between CDs and picric acid. The fluorescence measurement was recorded between the emission range of 300−700 nm. The selectivity of picric acid was studied by adding different organic compounds (Phenol, Aniline, nitrobenzene, 4-Nitrophenol, Hydroquinone, Benzoquinone, Toluene, Benzoic acid and Picric acid) with the concentration of 333μM and their fluorescence spectrum was taken at excitation wavelength of 320 nm by following the similar procedure. All the experiments were carried out at room temperature. The limit of detection (LOD) = 3σ/K. (where σ-standard deviation and K-Slope of the linear equation).

The detection of Fe3+ was done at room temperature using distilled water. In this procedure, calculated quantity of Fe3+ solution was added to 20 μL of prepared N-CDs solution and ultrasonicated for 5 min to enable complete interaction. After that the fluorescence measurement was recorded at excitation wavelength of 320 nm. To further, study the selectivity of synthesized N-CDs, different metal ions (333μM) were prepared and added instead of Fe3+ to NCDs solution and recorded in similar way.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation of CDs from betel leaves
ce:section.ce:para: The formation of carbon dots from betel leaves is explained by following steps: dehydration, polymerization, condensation, aromatization and nucleation. The first step involves the hydrothermal treatment of betel leaves which contains tannins, sugar, chavicol, essential oil etc., and liq.NH3 which undergoes decomposition and dehydration process when the temperature starts increasing from 100 °C. Later, the small molecules tend to align and form a polymer by the process of polymerization and condensation. Finally, the aromatization of these polymers, which undergoes nucleation and further growth process leads to the formation of N-CDs [33,34].

The surface morphology of synthesised CDs was examined through Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) analysis. The HR-TEM image of CDs was displayed in the Fig. 1 . It reveals that CDs are uniform, well distributed smaller particles having a size less 10 nm which are spherical in morphology without any aggregation Fig. 1(a–c). Furthermore, these results are supported and the size of CDs has been confirmed by particle size analyser (PSA) which shows the average particle of 3.7 nm with PDI value of 0.485 (Fig. 1(d)).

The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern exhibits diffused rings which indicates that the synthesized CDs are amorphous in nature (inset Fig. 1(b)) [35,36]. The AFM topographical image depicts that the CDs are smaller spherical particles and the histogram of CDs exhibits particle in size of below 10 nm (Fig. 2 (a, b)). The two dimensional image (2D) of CDs (Fig. 2(a) is displayed in three dimensional form in Fig. 2(c). The average roughness of the CDs was found to be 7.25 nm [37,38].

The optical properties of N-CDs were studied by UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy, displayed in the Fig. 3 . The UV–vis spectrum of N-CDs showed an intense absorption peak at 278 nm ascribed to the π-π* transition of sp2 hybrization and shoulder peak at 326 nm due to n- π* [39]. UV–vis absorption of CDs shows a strong absorption spectrum with extending tail in the visible region (Fig. 3(a)). The prepared carbon dots are pale yellow in color under visible light whereas it shows blue color under UV light (365 nm) which reveals the blue emission property (inset in Fig. 3(a)). The corresponding emission spectrum for the absorbance was displayed in the Fig. 3(b). The maximum fluorescence emission peak was found at 402 nm at excitation wavelength of 320 nm. Fig. 3(c) displays emission spectra of N-CDs at different excitation wavelength in the range of 300 nm–460 nm with the increment of 20 nm. Initially, the emission intensity increases with increasing λex, and the maximum emission intensity of CDs was obtained at 402 nm at excitation wavelength of 320 nm. Further increase in the excitation wavelength, there is change in emission intensity accompanied with a gradual red shift of the emission peak. This excitation dependent emission behavior may be due to presence of different surface functional sites as well as the particle size [40]. Though many efforts have been reported for fluorescence of CDs, but the origin of fluorescence of CDs still needs a discussion. The fluorescence of CDs is might be originated from combination of surface state and confinement effect. The excitation dependent emission of CDs is due to the surface defects produced by the surface oxidation and different size of the particles. On other hand, oxygen containing active groups produced from surface oxidation and nitrogen containing active functional group by doping process has strong influence on energy gap of surface state which reduces the band gap of CDs leads to red shifted fluorescence spectrum of CDs [41]. The fluorescence quantum yield of N-CDs was calculated to be 4.21 % using quinine sulphate as a standard.

The FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs was displayed in the Fig. 4 . The characteristic band exits between 3300−3600 cm−1 region corresponding to OH and NH stretching. The peak at 1631 cm−1 is ascribed to CONH and CO stretching vibration. The peaks around 1400, 1250 and 1100 cm−1 are corresponding to CN, CN and CO stretching respectively [30,42]. These results claim that presence of oxygen and nitrogen containing active functional site on their surface makes N-CDs more hydrophilic and enables ease dispersion in aqueous solution.

The structural analysis and purity of N-doped carbon dots were studied by X-ray diffraction measurements. The diffraction pattern of N-CDs showed a broad peak at 2θ = 24° corresponds to (002) plane (Fig. 5 ). The interlayer spacing value carbon dots is 3.7 Ao which is higher than that of bulk graphite (3.4 Ao), which explains the amorphous nature of carbon dots [43]. This characteristic amorphous nature might be arising from the closely packed carbon atoms and introduction of active functional sites on the surface of N-CDs [42]. These results claim that the betel leaves derived CDs are pure by exhibiting only the characteristic peak of carbon dots.

XPS measurement was performed in order to study the elemental composition and confirm the presence of surface functional groups of N-CDs. Fig. 6 (a) displays the full scan XPS spectra of CDs showing three peaks at 283.7 eV, 398.9 eV and 529.8 eV which are assigned to binding energy of C1s, N1s and O1s respectively. Further high resolution of C1s was deconvoluted and showing four different chemical environment at binding energy of 282.1 eV, 282.8 eV, 283.8 eV and 286 eV which are assigned to CC, CC, CN/CO and CO bond respectively (Fig. 6(b)). The O1s high resolution spectra displayed in the Fig. 6(c), displays two peaks at 529.1 eV and 530.3 eV which are attributed to CO and C—O bond [44]. The N1s spectra displays two peaks at 398 eV and 399.7 eV which exhibits the occurance of CNC and NC3 bond (Fig. 6(d)). All these results are in good agreement with obtained FTIR results, demonstrating the existence of active surface functional sites as well as confirm the successful doping of N element into the carbon dots [45,46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH, storage time and ionic strength
ce:section.ce:para: A set of samples was prepared with different pH ranging from 2−12. The influence of pH value on emission intensity of N-CDs was studied by adding different pH solution and fluorescence measurements were recorded. It showed negligible effect on emission intensity by the addition of different pH solution. It clearly indicates that the synthesized carbon dots are independent of pH value and therefore pH 7 could be considered as suitable pH for sensing studies (Fig. S1). As shown in the Fig. S2, the synthesized carbon dots exhibited an excellent stability as showing no significant change in the fluorescent intensity with incubation time. Further, ionic stability of synthesized CDs was tested with different concentration of NaCl ranging from 0 to 1 mol/L. As displayed in the Fig. S3, there is no evident change in the fluorescence intensity with NaCl concentration even at high dose of 1 mol/L which clearly confirms that the synthesized CDs possess excellent stability at high ionic strength [26].

The selectivity of the present sensor was evaluated by subjecting the carbon dots to different aromatic compounds such as phenol, nitrobenzene, aniline, benzoic acid, hydroquinone, p-benzoquinone, 4-nitrophenol, toluene, benzene and picric acid (Fig.7 ). Among all, PA exhibited a remarkable quenching efficiency whereas other compounds exhibit negligible effect except nitrobenzene, 4-nitrophenol and p-benzoquinone which showed a lesser quenching rate in the efficiency order of PA>4-Nitrophenol > Nitrobenzene > p-benzoquinone. Though 4-nitrophenol exhibited highest quenching efficiency next to picric acid, the presence of higher number of electron accepting nature of nitro groups on benzene in picric acid attributed to highest the quenching rate of picric acid. Moreover, the lower F/Fo value of PA is indicating that CDs is highly selective for picric acid detection than other investigated aromatic compounds.

The sensitivity of the proposed system has been studied by measuring the effect of the absorbance and emission intensity with different concentration of picric acid. The UV–vis spectrum of N-CDs was obtained with absence and presence of picric acid as displayed in the Fig. S4. It exhibits an evident change in the absorbance spectrum of N-CDs upon addition of picric acid. An enhancement in the absorbance value was achieved with increasing concentration of picric acid which is clearly indicating the electrostatic interaction between N-CDs and picric acid.

Fig. 8 (a) displays that the fluorescence intensity of carbon dots with increasing concentration of PA at excitation wavelength of 320 nm. The intensity of the emission spectrum was decreased gradually with increasing concentration of picric acid and exhibited slight shift (red shift) in emission spectrum towards longer wavelength at higher concentration of picric acid. This red shift confirms the electrostatic interaction between carbon dots and picric acid. Fig. 8(b) depicts the response between fluorescence intensity and concentration of PA in the ranging 0–33.3 μM. These results confirm that the present sensing probe is highly sensitive towards picric acid. Fig. 8(c) shows the linear response (R2 = 0.9923) between concentrations of picric acid and fluorescence intensity with limit of detection (LOD) 0.11μM in the concentration range 0.3−3.3 μM.

The possible mechanism of fluorescence quenching of N-CDs by picric acid is explained by the two ways such as FRET and molecular interaction (Scheme 2 ). There are certain rules which are required to implement that quenching mechanism is due to FRET. These rules are (i) the absorption spectrum of quencher (Picric acid) should overlap with emission spectrum of fluorophore. (ii) There is a change in fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs before and after the addition of quencher [30]. As shown in the Fig. S5, there is wide overlap between absorption spectrum of PA and emission spectrum of N-CDs which suggests that a reduction in fluorescence intensity might be due to FRET [47]. At same time, Fig. S6 shows the fluorescence lifetime spectrum N-CDs and Fig. S7 shows the lifetime measurement of CDs with addition of picric acid, which indicates that there is no change in the fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs before (5.177 ns) and after the addition of PA (5.174 ns) as displayed in the Table S1. These results suggest that quenching of fluorescence due to FRET was ruled out. Another reason for fluorescence quenching might be due to the molecular interaction exists between the CDs and PA which is explained by the oxygen and nitrogen based groups present on CDs interact with phenolic group of PA via electrostatic interaction or hydrogen bonding as result of quenching of fluorescence [48]. In addition, existence of greater number electron withdrawing characteristic of nitro groups on benzene can interact with nitrogen containing functional groups via electrostatic interaction leads to reduction in the fluorescence intensity of CDs. The presence of nitro groups also plays a vital role in quenching of fluorescence [49,50].

The selectivity of the proposed sensing system was studied towards the detection of metal ions. Different metal cations were prepared including K+, Cu+, Na+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Cr6+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Cd2+, Ag2+, Fe3+, Hg2+ and Mn2+ which were added to N-CDs, incubated for 5 min. and fluorescence spectra was taken under same conditions. From the spectra (Fig. 9 ), there is negligible effect was observed upon addition of different metal cations except Fe3+. The addition of Fe3+ showed a remarkable change in intensity of N-CDs due to coordination or chelation as result of the emission intensity gets quenched.

Later, competition experiment has been evaluated for CDs towards Fe3+ detection (Fig. S8). The CDs comprising different metal ions has been prepared in which Fe3+ solution was added and corresponding fluorescence spectrum was recorded. It displays comparable and same fluorescence quenching response as that of Fe3+ only. These results clearly reveal that the synthesized CDs could selectively recognize Fe3+ over the occurrence of other metal ions.

The possible mechanism for fluorescence quenching is explained by strong interaction exists between Fe3+ and surface groups of N-CDs (Scheme 3 ). Upon addition of Fe3+ into N-CDs, it binds with large number of OH, COOH and NH2 groups occupied on the surface of N-CDs as result of formation of chelation or coordination causes transfer of electron from excited state of N-CDs to partially filled d-orbital which facilitates the non-emissive electron/hole pair recombination as result of quenching of fluorescence [18,26].

Fig. 10 (a) shows the emission spectra at different concentration of Fe3+ ions. The sensitivity of the synthesized carbon dots was studied by adding different concentration of Fe3+ into CDs incubated for 5 min. and corresponding fluorescence measurement was recorded. Fig. it is clearly demonstrated that the emission intensity of as-synthesized N-CDs was gradually decreased with increasing concentration of Fe3+ due to interaction between surface functional sites of N-CDs and Fe3+ ions. This interaction is also evidenced by absorbance measurement. The UV–vis spectrum of N-CDs was taken in the absence and presence of Fe3+ (Fig. S9). It shows obvious change in absorbance value as well as a slight peak shift was observed upon addition of Fe3+. Fig. 10(b) displays the relation between emission intensity and concentration of Fe3+. It exhibits a good linear response R2 = 0.9918 (Fig. 10(c)) with limit of detection (LOD) = 0.135μM in the linear range of 0.3−3.3μM. Overall, the obtained results claim that the present sensing system is highly sensitive towards picric acids and Fe3+ ions. The obtained LOD values are comparable and lower than the previously reported literatures which is mentioned in the Table 1 .

In this present work, N-CDs were successfully synthesized from betel leaves for the first time and ammonia solution as a green nitrogen dopant by hydrothermal method. The adopted route is a green, cost effective, environment benign and perfectly suitable for large scale production. The synthesized CDs showed blue emission under UV light, excitation dependent emission behavior, water solubility, independent of pH and good storage stability. The synthesized probe exhibits dual detection (PA and Fe3+) with excellent selectivity and selectivity in connection with fluorescence quenching of CDs. The system shows a limit of detection 0.11 μM for PA and 0.135μM for Fe3+. The present dual sensing system is green, fast, cost effective, ecofriendly and potential candidate for detection of picric acid and Fe3+ ions in the environmental samples.
K. Kalanidhi - Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing.
Dr. P.Nagaraaj - Technical correction, Visualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors whose names K. Kalanidhi, P. Nagaraaj certify that their affiliation is Department of Chemistry, Anna University, Chennai-25. The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The author thanks to University Grants Commission, New Delhi, India for providing NFOBC fellowship and the authors also thank to Department of chemistry, Anna University for constant support and encouragement.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2021.113369.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Aggregation-induced phosphorescence quenching method for the detection of picric acid based on melamine-passivated Mn-doped ZnS quantum dots
Spectrochim. Acta A. Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Zhao, Z. Fan. Aggregation-induced phosphorescence quenching method for the detection of picric acid based on melamine-passivated Mn-doped ZnS quantum dots. Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy 223 (2019) 117323.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of 2, 4, 6-trinitrophenol by in-situ assembly of SBA-15 with multi-hydroxyl carbon dots
Anal. Chim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Wang, X. Li, X. Li, Y. Wang, Q. Han, J. Li. Determination of 2, 4, 6-trinitrophenol by in-situ assembly of SBA-15 with multi-hydroxyl carbon dots. Analytica Chimica Acta 1098 (2020) 170-80.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Pyridoxamine driven selective turn-off detection of picric acid using glutathione stabilized fluorescent copper nanoclusters and its applications with chemically modified cellulose strips
Biosens. Bioelectron.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Patel, S. Bothra, R. Kumar, G. Crisponi, SK. Sahoo. Pyridoxamine driven selective turn-off detection of picric acid using glutathione stabilized fluorescent copper nanoclusters and its applications with chemically modified cellulose strips. Biosensors and Bioelectronics 102 (2018) 196-203.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: All-inorganic perovskite quantum dots CsPbX3 (Br/I) for highly sensitive and selective detection of explosive picric acid
Chem. Eng. J.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Chen, C. Sun, Y. Liu, L. Yu, K. Zhang, AM. Asiri, HM. Marwani, H. Tan, Y. Ai, X. Wang, S. Wang. All-inorganic perovskite quantum dots CsPbX3 (Br/I) for highly sensitive and selective detection of explosive picric acid. Chemical Engineering Journal 379 (2020) 122360.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rapid synthesis of a hyperfluorescence 2-pyridone derivative as a fluorescent molecular sensor for picric acid
Sens. Actuators B: Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Luo, Y. Li, Y. Xu, S. Zhang, Y.Wang, X. Kou, D. Xiao. Rapid synthesis of a hyperfluorescence 2-pyridone derivative as a fluorescent molecular sensor for picric acid. Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical 253 (2017) 231-8.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One step hydrothermal synthesis of carbon nanodots to realize the fluorescence detection of picric acid in real samples
Sens. Actuators B: Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Li, L. Zhang, P. Li, Y. Zhang, C. Dong. One step hydrothermal synthesis of carbon nanodots to realize the fluorescence detection of picric acid in real samples. Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical 258 (2018) 580-8.
